role_summary: First cousin of Emmett Till (catalog external figure); son of Moses Wright, the great-uncle Till was visiting at the time of his murder; eyewitness to Till's August 28, 1955 abduction from the Wright home in Money, Mississippi; co-author of Simeon's Story: An Eyewitness Account of the Kidnapping of Emmett Till (2010); pastor of Northwestern Baptist Church in Cicero, Illinois.
ai_reading: Wright's top semantic neighbor is Wheeler Parker, Jr. (entry 125) at a high cosine count of 243, a strong single-neighbor signature in this catalog and the catalog's clearest fellow-witness vector pairing: Parker is also Till's cousin, was also present at the August 1955 Money, Mississippi events, and his oral history rhymes with Wright's testimony as the catalog's most-mutually-corroborating pair. On the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis the catalog places Wright at +0.75, far toward Individual Conscience and one of the most-distinctive structural readings in the catalog; for a single-event eyewitness whose primary biographical claim is the firsthand observation of his cousin's abduction, the embedding's reach toward Individual Conscience tracks a testimony register built on personal moral witness and the autobiography of what one boy saw at the foot of his own bed, rather than the organizational and committee-structured register of the Collective Discipline pole that draws many movement-organizer interviews.
biographical_paragraph: Simeon Wright's Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript. His external biographical record is well-documented in his 2010 memoir [src: 1]. Wright was born September 13, 1942 in Money, Mississippi, the youngest of Moses and Elizabeth Wright's twelve children; he was twelve years old in August 1955 when his cousin Emmett Till came down from Chicago to spend two weeks with the family. He was sleeping in the same bed as Till when Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam came to the Wright home in the early morning of August 28, 1955 and took Till at gunpoint. The Wright family fled Mississippi after the trial; Moses Wright had testified against Bryant and Milam (the photograph of his courtroom finger-pointing at the killers is one of the most-circulated images of the case), and the family settled in Argo, Illinois. Simeon Wright became a pipefitter and a pastor (Northwestern Baptist Church in Cicero, Illinois) and lived his adult life in the Chicago area. His 2010 memoir Simeon's Story: An Eyewitness Account of the Kidnapping of Emmett Till (with Herb Boyd, Lawrence Hill Books) is his canonical first-person account [src: 2]. He died September 4, 2017 at seventy-four [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Simeon Wright
interview_snippets.lead_in: Wright describes the sleeping arrangements in the Money, Mississippi farmhouse after his cousin Emmett Till and other relatives arrived for the visit in late August 1955:
interview_snippets.quote: And we talked, and then my mother decided who was going to sleep with who. Emmett and I shared a bed. Uh, in my bedroom was two beds.

interview_snippets.speaker: Simeon Wright
interview_snippets.lead_in: Wright recounts what he saw at Bryant's store on the evening of Wednesday, August 24, 1955, after his older brother Maurice sent him inside to watch Emmett Till:
interview_snippets.quote: He paid for his items, and we walked outside of the store. We're standing on the, that would be the south side of the door there. Carolyn [Bryant] came out, walking north towards her car. And before she can get off the little wooden walkway there, Emmett whistled at her.

interview_snippets.speaker: Simeon Wright
interview_snippets.lead_in: Wright describes waking in the early morning of August 28, 1955 as Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam entered the bedroom he shared with Till:
interview_snippets.quote: And I heard the noise, you know, the loud talking, and I woke up and saw these two white men standing at the foot of my bed. One had a gun, flashlight. Later on found out it was J. W. Milam. He ordered me to lay back down and go back to sleep. And he made Emmett get up and dress and marched him out to the truck.

interview_snippets.speaker: Simeon Wright
interview_snippets.lead_in: Wright recalls the voice that identified Till from the abductors' truck outside the house:
interview_snippets.quote: And once they marched him out to the truck, a lady's voice, she responded when they asked her, "Is this the right one?" A lady responded, "He is."

interview_snippets.speaker: Wheeler Parker, Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Wright's cousin Wheeler Parker, Jr., also present at Bryant's store that evening, places Simeon Wright at the counter just behind Till:
interview_snippets.quote: So I left Emmett in the store, and Simeon Wright came in right behind him.

gallery.alt: The surviving ruins of Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market in Money, Mississippi, photographed in 2018
gallery.caption: The surviving ruins of Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market in Money, Mississippi, photographed in 2018. This is the store Wright entered on the evening of August 24, 1955, sent in by his brother Maurice to watch his cousin Emmett Till; in his oral history Wright places himself and Till leaving the store together just before Till whistled at Carolyn Bryant outside. Photographed by Eames Heard.

sources.title: Simeon Wright oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Simeon Wright with Herb Boyd, Simeon's Story: An Eyewitness Account of the Kidnapping of Emmett Till
